message:
test(supply_chain): add RFC3339 timestamp format verification tests

Concurrent agent added tests confirming the chrono-based timestamp formatting
(committed in fd4f544) actually produces valid RFC3339 output and not plain
Unix seconds:

- category_shift.rs: format_iso_timestamp_uses_rfc3339_and_not_unix_seconds
  asserts chrono parses the output and it's not just "{secs}Z";
  report_generated_at_iso_format gains explicit RFC3339 parse assertion
- trust_card.rs: timestamp_from_secs_uses_rfc3339_and_not_unix_seconds
  with the same pattern

Beads tracker metadata updated concurrently.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-c97l","title":"[10.20] Integrate DGIS topological context into trust cards, adversary graph posterior updates, and extension risk UI.","description":"## Why This Exists

DGIS computes rich topological risk information (metrics, fragility, contagion simulations), but this intelligence is only useful if it reaches the operators who make trust decisions. This bead integrates DGIS topological context into three user-facing surfaces: trust cards (the primary extension risk display), adversary graph posterior updates (Bayesian threat model refinement), and the extension risk UI.

Trust cards must show node-level topological blast-radius context so operators understand not just "this extension is risky" but "compromising this extension would propagate to N downstream dependents via M paths." The adversary graph posterior must incorporate topology features (centrality, fragility, barrier status) as evidence that updates threat probability estimates with explicit attribution.

Within the 9N enhancement map, this bead is the primary user-facing integration point, making DGIS intelligence consumable by operators and automated decision-making systems downstream.

## What This Must Do

1. Integrate node-level topological blast-radius context into trust card data: for each extension, show downstream impact (transitive dependent count), upstream risk exposure (transitive dependency risk aggregation), and articulation-point status.
2. Show delta impact from planned updates: before/after topology risk scores for proposed dependency changes.
3. Update adversary graph posteriors with topology features: incorporate centrality, fragility class, barrier status, and contagion simulation results as evidence in Bayesian posterior computation.
4. Provide explicit attribution: for each posterior update, show which topology features contributed and by how much.
5. Render risk UI surfaces with interpretable visualizations: blast-radius overlays, risk-delta heatmaps, and attribution breakdowns.
6. Ensure integration is backward-compatible: trust cards without DGIS context continue to function (graceful degradation).

## Acceptance Criteria

- Risk surfaces show node-level topological blast-radius context and delta impact from planned updates; posterior scoring incorporates topology features with explicit attribution.
- Trust cards include transitive dependent count, upstream risk aggregation, and articulation-point indicators.
- Delta impact display shows before/after risk scores for at least 3 types of planned changes (add dependency, update version, remove dependency).
- Posterior attribution includes per-feature contribution percentages for topology features.
- Graceful degradation: trust cards render correctly when DGIS data is unavailable.

## Testing & Logging Requirements

- Unit tests: trust card data assembly from topology metrics; delta impact computation for add/update/remove scenarios; posterior update with topology features; attribution calculation correctness.
- Integration tests: full pipeline from ingested graph + metrics to rendered trust card data; posterior update verification against known Bayesian computation fixtures; graceful degradation when DGIS data is absent.
- Structured logging: integration events with stable codes (DGIS-TRUSTCARD-001 through DGIS-TRUSTCARD-NNN); posterior update telemetry with feature attributions; trace correlation IDs.
- Deterministic replay: trust card snapshot fixtures for UI regression testing.

## Expected Artifacts

- `src/security/dgis/risk_surface_integration.rs` -- integration implementation
- `tests/integration/dgis_trust_card_integration.rs` -- integration test suite
- `artifacts/10.20/dgis_risk_ui_snapshot.json` -- sample risk UI snapshot
- `artifacts/section_10_20/bd-c97l/verification_evidence.json` -- machine-readable CI/release gate evidence
- `artifacts/section_10_20/bd-c97l/verification_summary.md` -- human-readable verification summary

## Dependencies

- bd-t89w (blocks) -- [10.20] Implement topological risk metric engine: provides the metrics that are integrated into trust cards and posteriors","acceptance_criteria":"- Risk surfaces show node-level topological blast-radius context and delta impact from planned updates; posterior scoring incorporates topology features with explicit attribution.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:37:06.997270565Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-22T07:08:22.182533619Z","closed_at":"2026-02-22T07:08:22.182494456Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-10-20","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-c97l","depends_on_id":"bd-t89w","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T17:05:04.514535866Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-cclm","title":"[10.20] Add adversarial validation suite (graph poisoning, edge-obfuscation, fake-low-risk pivots, delayed activation) with fail-closed semantics.","description":"## Why This Exists

A defense system that has never been tested against realistic attacks provides false confidence. This bead adds the adversarial validation suite for DGIS -- a comprehensive red-team test harness that attempts to defeat DGIS protections using realistic attack techniques: graph poisoning (injecting false edges/nodes), edge-obfuscation (hiding real dependencies behind indirection), fake-low-risk pivots (making high-risk nodes appear benign), and delayed activation (compromises that remain dormant through initial scanning).

The suite operates with fail-closed semantics: any attack that bypasses DGIS detection without generating at least a bounded-damage warning represents a validation failure that blocks release. This ensures DGIS claims about security coverage are backed by adversarial evidence.

Within the 9N enhancement map, this is the adversarial quality assurance layer that validates DGIS effectiveness against realistic threat models before claims are made in release artifacts.

## What This Must Do

1. Encode adversarial campaigns as deterministic fixtures: each campaign is a reproducible sequence of graph modifications and attack actions.
2. Implement graph poisoning campaigns: inject false edges, phantom nodes, and fabricated provenance to test ingestion resilience.
3. Implement edge-obfuscation campaigns: hide real dependency edges behind dynamic loading, code generation, or transitive indirection.
4. Implement fake-low-risk pivot campaigns: manipulate node properties to make high-centrality attacker-controlled nodes appear benign.
5. Implement delayed-activation campaigns: compromises that pass initial scanning but activate after a configurable dormancy period.
6. Verify fail-closed semantics: DGIS must detect or bound damage within defined limits for each campaign type.
7. Emit stable error classes for bypass attempts with structured remediation hints.
8. Block release claims when adversarial campaigns achieve unbounded bypass.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Adversarial campaigns are encoded as deterministic fixtures; DGIS detects or bounds damage within defined limits; bypass attempts emit stable error classes and remediation hints.
- At least 4 campaign types are implemented: graph poisoning, edge-obfuscation, fake-low-risk pivots, delayed activation.
- Each campaign is deterministically reproducible from its fixture descriptor.
- Fail-closed: bypass detection rate meets defined minimum thresholds per campaign type.
- Error classes are stable across runs and include structured remediation hints.
- Release gating: adversarial results are consumed by the section gate (bd-3po7).

## Testing & Logging Requirements

- Unit tests: individual campaign generation and fixture determinism; attack action application to test graphs; detection/bypass outcome classification.
- Integration tests: full adversarial suite execution against DGIS pipeline; fail-closed verification for each campaign type; bypass rate measurement; remediation hint generation.
- Structured logging: adversarial events with stable codes (DGIS-ADVERSARY-001 through DGIS-ADVERSARY-NNN); per-campaign detection/bypass telemetry; remediation hints; trace correlation IDs.
- Deterministic replay: adversarial campaign fixtures are the primary reproducibility mechanism.

## Expected Artifacts

- `tests/security/dgis_adversarial_suite.rs` -- adversarial test suite
- `docs/security/dgis_attack_playbook.md` -- attack playbook documentation
- `artifacts/10.20/dgis_adversarial_results.json` -- adversarial validation results
- `artifacts/section_10_20/bd-cclm/verification_evidence.json` -- machine-readable CI/release gate evidence
- `artifacts/section_10_20/bd-cclm/verification_summary.md` -- human-readable verification summary

## Dependencies

- bd-2bj4 (blocks) -- [10.20] Implement deterministic graph ingestion pipeline: adversarial campaigns test the ingestion pipeline's resilience to malicious inputs","acceptance_criteria":"- Adversarial campaigns are encoded as deterministic fixtures; DGIS detects or bounds damage within defined limits; bypass attempts emit stable error classes and remediation hints.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:37:07.527097073Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-22T07:08:21.612241901Z","closed_at":"2026-02-22T07:08:21.612211845Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-10-20","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-cclm","depends_on_id":"bd-2bj4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T17:05:13.841930056Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-cda","title":"[PLAN 10.N] Execution Normalization Contract (No Duplicate Implementations)","description":"Section: 10.N — Execution Normalization Contract (No Duplicate Implementations)

Strategic Context:
Execution normalization to prevent duplicate implementations, enforce canonical ownership, and keep cross-track integration coherent.

Execution Requirements:
- Preserve all scoped capabilities from the canonical plan.
- Keep contracts self-contained so future contributors can execute without reopening the master plan.
- Require explicit testing strategy (unit + integration/e2e) and structured logging/telemetry for every child bead.
- Require artifact-backed validation for performance, security, and correctness claims.

Dependency Semantics:
- This epic is blocked by its child implementation beads.
- Cross-epic dependencies encode strategic sequencing and canonical ownership boundaries.

## Success Criteria
- All child beads for this section are completed with acceptance artifacts attached and no unresolved blockers.
- Section-level verification gate for comprehensive unit tests, integration/e2e workflows, and detailed structured logging evidence is green.
- Scope-to-plan traceability is explicit, with no feature loss versus the canonical plan section.

## Optimization Notes
- User-Outcome Lens: "[PLAN 10.N] Execution Normalization Contract (No Duplicate Implementations)" must improve operator confidence, safety posture, and deterministic recovery behavior under both normal and adversarial conditions.
- Cross-Section Coordination: child beads must encode integration assumptions explicitly to avoid local optimizations that degrade system-wide correctness.
- Verification Non-Negotiable: completion requires reproducible unit/integration/E2E evidence and structured logs suitable for independent replay.
- Scope Protection: any simplification must preserve canonical-plan feature intent and be justified with explicit tradeoff documentation.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:36:40.132488047Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-20T08:41:50.891275800Z","closed_at":"2026-02-20T08:41:50.891190381Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-10-N"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-cda","depends_on_id":"bd-1neb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:48:27.220550663Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-cda","depends_on_id":"bd-1oyt","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:50:04.664351830Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-cda","depends_on_id":"bd-1v2c","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:50:04.758779969Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-cda","depends_on_id":"bd-2yhs","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:50:04.569309366Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-cda","depends_on_id":"bd-zxk8","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:50:04.448172801Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-ck2h","title":"[10.13] Define MVP vs Full conformance profile matrix and publication claim rules.","description":"Master Plan Source: PLAN_TO_CREATE_FRANKEN_NODE.md
Section: 10.13 — FCP Deep-Mined Expansion Execution Track (9I)

Why This Exists:
Deep connector/provider contract track: lifecycle FSMs, conformance harnesses, fencing, sandboxing, revocation freshness, and protocol hardening.

Task Objective:
Define MVP vs Full conformance profile matrix and publication claim rules.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Profile matrix maps required capabilities to claim language; publication metadata is generated from measured profile results; unsupported claims are blocked.

Expected Artifacts:
- `docs/conformance/profile_matrix.md`, `tests/conformance/profile_claim_gate.rs`, `artifacts/10.13/profile_claim_report.json`.

- Machine-readable verification artifact at `artifacts/section_10_13/bd-ck2h/verification_evidence.json` for CI/release gating.
- Human-readable verification summary at `artifacts/section_10_13/bd-ck2h/verification_summary.md` linking implementation intent to measured outcomes.
Testing & Logging Requirements:
- Unit tests for core logic, invariants, and error handling.
- Integration/E2E tests for end-to-end control-flow behavior.
- Detailed structured logs/telemetry with stable codes and trace correlation IDs.
- Deterministic replay fixture or reproducible artifact for failure analysis.

Task-Specific Clarification:
- For "[10.13] Define MVP vs Full conformance profile matrix and publication claim rules.", preserve full capability scope from the plan; no feature compression or silent omission is allowed.
- Verification for "[10.13] Define MVP vs Full conformance profile matrix and publication claim rules." must include capability-specific thresholds and machine-readable pass/fail evidence artifacts.
- Unit tests, integration/E2E scripts, and structured logs for "[10.13] Define MVP vs Full conformance profile matrix and publication claim rules." must support deterministic replay and root-cause triage without hidden context.

Why This Improves User Outcomes:
- "[10.13] Define MVP vs Full conformance profile matrix and publication claim rules." is required to reduce operator error, increase deterministic safety guarantees, and improve time-to-diagnosis for failures in real-world workflows.
- Explicit success/failure evidence for "[10.13] Define MVP vs Full conformance profile matrix and publication claim rules." prevents false confidence and improves trust in migration/compatibility/security outcomes.
- Detailed unit + integration/E2E + structured logging requirements ensure reproducible quality and faster incident remediation for users.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:36:54.818421576Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-20T13:29:24.402952603Z","closed_at":"2026-02-20T13:29:24.402923880Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-10-13","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-ck2h","depends_on_id":"bd-1gnb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:43:14.020689699Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-cuut","title":"[10.15] Define lane mapping policy for control-plane workloads (Cancel/Timed/Ready).","description":"## Why This Exists
Hard Runtime Invariant #5 from Section 8.5 requires scheduler lane discipline: every control-plane task must be assigned to a lane (Cancel, Timed, or Ready) with an explicit priority and budget policy, and starvation across lanes must be detected and prevented. Without lane discipline, a flood of Ready-lane background tasks can starve Cancel-lane shutdown handlers, causing cancellation timeouts and quiescence violations. This bead defines the lane mapping policy for all control-plane workload classes in franken_node's product layer, building on the canonical scheduler lane infrastructure established in Section 10.14 (bd-qlc6).

## What This Must Do
1. Author `docs/specs/control_lane_mapping.md` defining:
   - The three lane classes: **Cancel** (cancellation handlers, drain operations, region close — highest priority, guaranteed minimum budget), **Timed** (health checks, lease renewals, epoch transitions — deadline-bound with timeout enforcement), **Ready** (background maintenance, telemetry flush, evidence archival — best-effort with starvation floor).
   - Per-task-class lane assignments: map every control-plane task type from the workflow inventory (bd-2177) to its lane.
   - Budget policies: Cancel lane gets at least 20% of scheduler capacity; Timed lane gets at least 30%; Ready lane gets remainder.
   - Starvation detection thresholds: if any lane receives zero scheduling slots for N consecutive ticks, emit a starvation alert.
2. Implement `tests/conformance/control_lane_policy.rs` that:
   - Asserts every task class in the control-plane module has a lane assignment in the policy document.
   - Validates budget allocations sum correctly.
   - Simulates a workload mix and asserts no lane is starved (receives zero slots for more than the threshold).
   - Validates that Cancel-lane tasks are scheduled before Ready-lane tasks when both are pending.
3. Generate `artifacts/10.15/lane_starvation_metrics.csv` with columns: `tick, cancel_lane_tasks_run, timed_lane_tasks_run, ready_lane_tasks_run, cancel_lane_starved, timed_lane_starved, ready_lane_starved`.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Every control task class has lane assignment and budget policy; starvation checks are automated.
- Cancel-lane tasks are never starved for more than 1 tick (immediate priority).
- Budget allocations are enforced in conformance tests with synthetic workloads.
- Lane assignments are machine-readable and consumed by the scheduler integration (from 10.14's bd-qlc6).
- Starvation metrics CSV is consumed by observability dashboards (bd-3gnh).

## Testing & Logging Requirements
- **Unit tests**: Validate lane assignment lookup, budget arithmetic, and starvation threshold detection with synthetic task queues.
- **Integration tests**: Run a mixed workload (cancel + timed + ready tasks) through the scheduler and assert lane discipline.
- **Conformance tests**: Flood the Ready lane and assert Cancel-lane tasks still execute within their budget.
- **Adversarial tests**: Submit cancel-lane tasks that exceed their budget; assert they are preempted. Submit a workload that would starve the Timed lane; assert starvation alert fires.
- **Structured logs**: Event codes `LAN-001` (task assigned to lane), `LAN-002` (lane budget enforced), `LAN-003` (starvation detected), `LAN-004` (starvation resolved), `LAN-005` (task preempted for lane budget). Include task_id, lane_class, budget_remaining_ms, and trace correlation ID.

## Expected Artifacts
- `docs/specs/control_lane_mapping.md`
- `tests/conformance/control_lane_policy.rs`
- `artifacts/10.15/lane_starvation_metrics.csv`
- `artifacts/section_10_15/bd-cuut/verification_evidence.json`
- `artifacts/section_10_15/bd-cuut/verification_summary.md`

## Dependencies
- **Upstream**: bd-qlc6 (10.14 — canonical scheduler lane infrastructure that this policy builds on)
- **Downstream**: bd-20eg (section gate), bd-lus (10.11 adopts lane policies for product operations)","acceptance_criteria":"- Every control task class has lane assignment and budget policy; starvation checks are automated.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:37:00.053329409Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-22T01:59:58.378429176Z","closed_at":"2026-02-22T01:59:58.378401494Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-10-15","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-cuut","depends_on_id":"bd-qlc6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T14:59:48.249813942Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-cv49","title":"[15] Adoption target: published security/ops improvement case studies","description":"Master Plan Source: PLAN_TO_CREATE_FRANKEN_NODE.md
Section: 15

Task Objective:
Deliver deterministic migration validation and publish measurable security/ops case studies.

Execution Requirements:
- Make this requirement machine-verifiable and release-gated where applicable.
- Keep behavior self-documenting so future contributors do not need to re-open the master plan.

Testing & Logging Requirements:
- Unit tests for rule/contract logic and error conditions.
- Integration/E2E tests for workflow-level enforcement.
- Structured logs with stable codes and trace IDs.
- Reproducible artifacts that prove contract satisfaction.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Success and failure invariants for [15] Adoption target: published security/ops improvement case studies are explicitly quantified and machine-verifiable.
- Determinism requirements for [15] Adoption target: published security/ops improvement case studies are validated across repeated runs and adversarial perturbations.


Expected Artifacts:
- Machine-readable verification artifact with explicit canonical path under artifacts/section_15/bd-cv49/verification_evidence.json, suitable for CI/release gating.
- Human-readable verification summary with explicit canonical path under artifacts/section_15/bd-cv49/verification_summary.md, linking implementation intent to measured validation outcomes.

Task-Specific Clarification:
- The implementation must deliver the exact capability named in the title, with no scope dilution and no silent feature omission.
- Validation artifacts must include capability-specific thresholds, pass/fail criteria, and reproducible evidence bundles tied to this bead ID.
- Program/section verification gates must be able to consume this bead's outputs without manual interpretation.

Why This Improves User Outcomes:
- "[15] Adoption target: published security/ops improvement case studies" is required to reduce operator error, increase deterministic safety guarantees, and improve time-to-diagnosis for failures in real-world workflows.
- Explicit success/failure evidence for "[15] Adoption target: published security/ops improvement case studies" prevents false confidence and improves trust in migration/compatibility/security outcomes.
- Detailed unit + integration/E2E + structured logging requirements ensure reproducible quality and faster incident remediation for users.","acceptance_criteria":"1. At least 3 case studies published documenting real-world security and operational improvements from adopting franken_node.
2. Each case study includes: (a) organization context (size, industry, anonymized if needed), (b) pre-adoption security posture metrics, (c) post-adoption security posture metrics, (d) operational improvements (incident response time, deployment frequency, etc.), (e) migration effort and timeline, (f) lessons learned and recommendations.
3. Quantitative improvements documented: at least 2 case studies show measurable security improvement (e.g., X% reduction in vulnerabilities, Y% faster incident containment).
4. Case studies are reviewed by the featured organization before publication.
5. Case studies are published on project website and submitted to at least 1 industry publication or conference.
6. Case study template exists for partners to self-author with editorial support.
7. Evidence: case_study_registry.json with per-study: title, organization type, key metrics, publication status, and URL.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"NavyPeak","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:39:36.696204017Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-22T01:05:06.917484491Z","closed_at":"2026-02-22T01:05:06.917448965Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-15","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-cv49","depends_on_id":"bd-elog","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:43:26.472641055Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
